metadata.unit: The Merry Adventures of Robin Hood
metadata.note: Complete curriculum data: informational texts, grammar, language, writing, and journal prompts for all 24 chapters

grammarLessons.instruction: A simple sentence has one independent clause. A compound sentence joins two independent clauses with a conjunction (and, but, or, so). A complex sentence has one independent clause and one dependent clause.
examples: Compound: Robin fled into the forest, and the foresters chased him.
Complex: When Robin killed the deer, he committed a crime.

grammarLessons.instruction: Every sentence has a subject (who or what the sentence is about) and a predicate (what the subject does or is).
examples: The young outlaw → subject | gathered loyal followers → predicate

grammarLessons.instruction: A direct object receives the action of the verb. An indirect object tells to whom or for whom the action is done.
examples: Robin gave Little John a place in the band. (indirect: Little John, direct: a place)

grammarLessons.instruction: An independent clause can stand alone. A dependent clause cannot.
examples: Independent: The Sheriff held a contest.
Dependent: when Robin appeared in disguise

grammarLessons.topic: Comma Rules - Items in a Series
grammarLessons.instruction: Use commas to separate three or more items in a series.
examples: Robin, Little John, and Will Scarlet stood together.

grammarLessons.instruction: Adjectives describe nouns. Adverbs describe verbs, adjectives, or other adverbs.
Past tense shows completed action. Present shows action happening now. Future shows action that will happen.
Quotation marks show exact words. Commas and periods go inside.
Pronouns take the place of nouns. Antecedents are the nouns they refer to.
examples: Allan loved Ellen. He wanted to marry her.

grammarLessons.instruction: Possessive nouns show ownership. Add 's to singular, ' to plural ending in s.
A run-on sentence joins two independent clauses without proper punctuation. A fragment is an incomplete sentence.
examples: Run-on: Robin fought Guy the battle was fierce (fix: add comma + and, or use semicolon)
Fragment: Because Guy was dangerous (fix: complete the thought)

grammarLessons.instruction: Semicolons join related independent clauses. Colons introduce lists or explanations.
examples: Semicolon: Robin loved Marian; she loved him.
Colon: Robin had one secret: Marian.

languageLessons.instruction: Context clues help you figure out unknown words. Look for definitions, examples, or synonyms nearby.
examples: Robin's temper flared—a weakness that would follow him

languageLessons.instruction: Prefixes change word meaning: un- (not), re- (again), pre- (before), dis- (not), mis- (wrongly).
Synonyms have similar meanings. Antonyms have opposite meanings.
Some words have multiple meanings depending on context.
examples: will: (1) person's name, (2) future helper word
Little John was strong as an ox.

languageLessons.instruction: Metaphors compare WITHOUT "like" or "as." Says one thing IS another.
Idioms mean something different from the literal words.
Many words come from Greek/Latin roots.
Homophones sound the same but have different meanings and spellings.
Denotation is the dictionary definition. Connotation is the emotional feeling a word carries.
examples: Both "proud" and "arrogant" mean confident, but arrogant has negative connotation

languageLessons.instruction: Analogies show relationships between words: A is to B as C is to D.
examples: Robin is to Marian as Allan is to Ellen (both are couples)

informationalTexts.title: Medieval English Law
informationalTexts.text:
In medieval England, forests belonged to the king. Hunting the king's deer was considered poaching—a crime punishable by death or mutilation. Common people could starve while surrounded by game they weren't allowed to touch. These harsh forest laws created widespread resentment and drove many otherwise law-abiding people to become outlaws. Robin Hood's story reflects this historical reality: one moment of impulse could turn a free person into a hunted criminal with no way back.

The foresters who enforced these laws were feared and hated. They had the power to arrest, fine, or even execute poachers on the spot. Their corruption was legendary—many took bribes or falsely accused innocent people to collect rewards. This system of injustice is why Robin Hood became a folk hero: he represented resistance against laws that protected the wealthy while oppressing the poor.

informationalTexts.title: Life in Sherwood Forest
informationalTexts.text:
Sherwood Forest in the 12th-13th centuries was an enormous woodland covering over 100,000 acres in Nottinghamshire. The forest was dense with oak, birch, and ash trees, providing perfect cover for those hiding from the law. Wild game was plentiful: deer, boar, rabbits, and birds. For outlaws, the forest offered both sanctuary and survival.

Living as an outlaw meant constant danger. You couldn't seek help from the law, visit family, or trade in towns. Outlaws formed bands for protection and survival. They built hidden camps, developed coded signals, and relied on a network of sympathetic villagers for information. The greenwood became a kingdom unto itself—outside royal law but governed by its own code of honor.

informationalTexts.title: Medieval Weapons: The Quarterstaff
informationalTexts.text:
The quarterstaff was one of the most common weapons in medieval England. Made from hardwood (usually oak or ash), it measured 6-8 feet long and was thick as a broom handle. Unlike swords, which only nobles could afford, anyone could make or own a quarterstaff. This made it the weapon of common people—farmers, travelers, and outlaws.

Fighting with a quarterstaff required skill, strength, and quick reflexes. Fighters used both ends of the staff, striking from multiple angles. A good quarterstaff fighter could defeat a swordsman by keeping them at distance and striking before they could close in. Little John's mastery of this weapon made him nearly unbeatable in close combat.

informationalTexts.title: Archery Contests in Medieval England
informationalTexts.text:
Archery contests were popular entertainment in medieval England. Kings and nobles held competitions to identify skilled archers for military service. The longbow was England's most powerful weapon, capable of penetrating armor at 200 yards. Archers trained from childhood to develop the strength needed to draw a 100-150 pound bow.

Prizes at archery contests included money, livestock, or valuable items like golden arrows. These contests drew competitors from across the country. Sheriffs and lords used them as recruiting opportunities—and sometimes as traps to catch wanted criminals who couldn't resist showing off their skills.

informationalTexts.title: Medieval Social Classes
informationalTexts.text:
Medieval English society was rigidly divided into classes. At the top were the king and nobles who owned land. Below them were knights who served the nobles in exchange for land and protection. The vast majority of people were peasants—farmers who worked the land but didn't own it.

Nobles like Will Scarlet's father could lose everything if a more powerful lord wanted their land. Corrupt officials used legal tricks, false debts, and bribery to seize property. Once wealthy, a family could become destitute overnight with no legal recourse. This injustice drove many educated, skilled people to join outlaw bands—bringing literacy, strategy, and fighting skills to the greenwood.

informationalTexts.title: Medieval Trades: The Tinker
informationalTexts.text:
Tinkers were traveling craftsmen who mended metal pots, pans, and tools. They moved from village to village, carrying their tools and supplies on their backs or in carts. Tinkers were skilled metalworkers who could repair almost anything, making them valuable in communities where buying new items was expensive.

Because they traveled constantly, tinkers heard news and gossip from many places. Some worked as messengers or spies. Their freedom to move without suspicion made them useful to both outlaws and authorities. A tinker carrying a warrant was a perfect cover—who would suspect a simple tradesman of working for the sheriff?

informationalTexts.title: Medieval Monasteries and Friars
informationalTexts.text:
In medieval England, monasteries were centers of learning, medicine, and hospitality. Monks and friars took vows of poverty, chastity, and obedience. Friars, unlike monks, traveled and preached among common people rather than staying in cloistered monasteries.

However, not all religious men were holy. Some abbots and bishops grew wealthy and corrupt, forgetting their vows. Friars who spoke out against this corruption were sometimes cast out of the church. These "rogue friars" often had more in common with outlaws than with church officials. Friar Tuck represents this type: a genuine man of faith who couldn't stomach the church's hypocrisy.

informationalTexts.title: Medieval Disguises
informationalTexts.text:
In an age before photographs or widespread literacy, disguises were remarkably effective. Most people couldn't read wanted posters, and descriptions were vague: "tall," "dark-haired," "bearded." Changing clothes, posture, and accent could make someone unrecognizable.

Successful disguises required more than costume—they required acting. Robin Hood was a master of this. He could mimic the speech patterns, mannerisms, and attitudes of different social classes. A beggar walked differently than a merchant. A butcher talked differently than a knight. By observing carefully and practicing, Robin could become anyone.

informationalTexts.title: Medieval Marriage and Debt
informationalTexts.text:
In medieval England, marriage among wealthy families was about property and alliances, not love. Fathers arranged their daughters' marriages to benefit the family. Young women had almost no say in whom they married. Refusing could mean disinheritance or worse.

Debt was a powerful weapon. A wealthy man could loan money at high interest, knowing the borrower could never repay. When debt came due, the lender could demand land, possessions, or even a family member in marriage. This practice was legal but deeply immoral—which is why stories like Allan-a-Dale's wedding rescue resonated with common people who had seen it happen.

informationalTexts.title: Sheriffs in Medieval England
informationalTexts.text:
Sheriffs were the king's representatives in each county, responsible for collecting taxes, enforcing laws, and maintaining order. It was one of the most powerful positions in medieval England—and one of the most corrupt. Sheriffs could enrich themselves by overtaxing, accepting bribes, and seizing property.

The Sheriff of Nottingham in Robin Hood tales represents this corruption. He persecutes outlaws not from duty but from wounded pride and desire for reward money. He uses his legal authority for personal vengeance. This made him the perfect villain in folk stories: someone with legitimate power who uses it unjustly.

informationalTexts.title: Medieval Assassins
informationalTexts.text:
Medieval England had professional fighters-for-hire called "knightly mercenaries." These were skilled warriors who fought for whoever paid them. Some were former knights who had lost their lands or been disgraced. With no loyalty except to money, they were dangerous and ruthless.

Guy of Gisbourne represents this type: a skilled fighter with no moral compass. The text describes him wearing horsehide (an unusual choice that suggests wildness) and carrying weapons designed only for killing. Unlike Robin's band, who rob but rarely kill, Guy has no code. This makes him more dangerous than any sheriff's army.

informationalTexts.title: Women in Medieval England
informationalTexts.text:
Medieval women were expected to be obedient daughters, then obedient wives. They couldn't own property, make contracts, or hold most positions of power. Education was rare for women except in noble families. A woman's value was often measured by her father's wealth or her ability to produce sons.

But reality was more complex than the rules. Some women managed estates while husbands were away at war. Others ran businesses, practiced medicine, or influenced politics indirectly. Maid Marian represents an idealized version of this: a woman who refuses to be limited by society's expectations and proves herself equal to any man.

informationalTexts.title: The Medieval Church and Taxes
informationalTexts.text:
The Catholic Church was immensely powerful and wealthy in medieval England. It collected tithes (10% of everyone's income) and owned vast amounts of land. Bishops lived like nobles, with castles, servants, and private armies. This wealth came from the poorest people, who were taught that giving to the church ensured salvation.

Many religious leaders were genuinely devoted, but corruption was widespread. Some bishops taxed ruthlessly, forgave sins for payment, and lived in luxury while preaching poverty. This hypocrisy angered common people and made corrupt churchmen targets for outlaws like Robin Hood, who saw them as thieves in holy robes.

informationalTexts.title: Medieval Knights and Honor
informationalTexts.text:
Knights were mounted warriors who served nobles in exchange for land. They trained from childhood in combat and followed a code of chivalry: protect the weak, keep your word, show courage, and serve your lord faithfully. In theory, knights were noble warriors. In practice, many were brutal and self-serving.

Sir Richard of the Lea represents the ideal: an honest knight who fell victim to circumstances beyond his control. His story shows how the medieval system could destroy even honorable people through debt and corrupt courts. Robin's help restores what should never have been taken—justice that the legal system failed to provide.

informationalTexts.title: Medieval Pride and Downfall
informationalTexts.text:
In medieval thinking, pride was one of the seven deadly sins. It meant excessive belief in your own abilities and importance. Pride led people to take foolish risks, refuse good advice, and make enemies unnecessarily. It was considered especially dangerous because proud people didn't recognize their own flaw.

Robin Hood's pride in his archery is both his greatest strength and his most dangerous weakness. It makes him the best archer in England—but it also makes him reckless. He takes unnecessary risks to prove his skill, ignoring warnings from wiser friends. The text shows that even heroes have flaws, and those flaws have consequences.

informationalTexts.title: King Richard the Lionheart
informationalTexts.text:
Richard I (1189-1199) was king of England but spent only six months of his ten-year reign in the country. He was a legendary warrior who led the Third Crusade, fought to reclaim his throne, and died in battle. During his absence, England was poorly governed by corrupt officials and his scheming brother John.

Richard's return in Robin Hood stories represents the fantasy of a just king setting things right. In reality, Richard cared little about England except as a source of money for his wars. But the legend of Richard as the "good king" contrasted with "bad king John" became a powerful part of English folklore.

informationalTexts.title: Medieval Pardons
informationalTexts.text:
Kings had the power to pardon criminals—even those guilty of serious crimes. Pardons were sometimes granted as rewards for service, sometimes sold for money, and sometimes given to win popularity. A royal pardon could restore citizenship, property, and legal rights.

But pardons came with expectations. A pardoned outlaw owed the king loyalty and service. Refusing could be seen as treason. Robin's pardon puts him in an impossible position: accept and lose his freedom, or refuse and insult the king. The story shows that even well-intentioned authority can trap those it claims to help.

informationalTexts.title: Medieval Court Life
informationalTexts.text:
Royal courts were centers of power, intrigue, and complex social rules. Courtiers competed for the king's favor through flattery, gifts, and political maneuvering. Success at court required education, refined manners, fashionable clothes, and the ability to navigate complex social hierarchies.

For someone like Robin Hood—used to the freedom and honesty of the greenwood—court life was suffocating. Every word was calculated. Enemies wore smiles. Decisions were made through whispered conversations rather than open debate. The text shows that physical chains aren't the only kind of prison.

informationalTexts.title: Medieval Freedom and Happiness
informationalTexts.text:
In medieval England, most people were tied to the land where they were born. Serfs couldn't leave their lord's estate without permission. Even freemen needed permission to travel or change occupations. "Freedom" meant something different than it does today.

Robin Hood's band represents a radical idea: freedom matters more than security or wealth. They choose danger and hardship in the forest over safety and servitude in society. The story asks: What makes a good life? Is it better to be safe and controlled, or free and at risk?

informationalTexts.title: Medieval Warfare Tactics
informationalTexts.text:
Medieval armies fought in formation with heavy cavalry and infantry. But forest warfare was different. In dense woods, horses were useless, formations couldn't form, and visibility was poor. This favored defenders who knew the terrain.

Robin's band used guerrilla tactics: ambushes, hit-and-run attacks, hidden pits, rope traps, and arrows from concealment. They fought only when conditions favored them and melted away when they didn't. This style of warfare frustrated traditional armies and made even small bands of outlaws difficult to defeat.

informationalTexts.title: Medieval Betrayal and Trust
informationalTexts.text:
In medieval society, trust was based on oaths and honor. People swore loyalty to lords, who promised protection in return. Breaking these oaths was the worst crime—worse than theft or violence. A traitor was despised because they destroyed the social bonds that held communities together.

Robin's band operated on trust. They had no legal contracts, no written agreements—only their word. Roger's betrayal shatters this foundation. It shows that even the most careful leader can be deceived, and that rebuilding trust after betrayal is nearly impossible.

informationalTexts.title: Medieval Medicine: Bloodletting
informationalTexts.text:
Medieval medicine was based on the theory of "humors"—four bodily fluids (blood, phlegm, yellow bile, black bile) that needed to be balanced. Bloodletting was a common treatment: cutting a vein to release "excess" blood, which supposedly cured fevers, infections, and other ailments.

In reality, bloodletting often did more harm than good, especially when patients were already weak. The Prioress's use of bloodletting to murder Robin while claiming to heal him shows how medical procedures could be weapons. Patients trusted healers completely, making them vulnerable to betrayal.

informationalTexts.title: Medieval Death and Burial
informationalTexts.text:
In medieval England, death rituals were deeply important. Bodies were prepared by family, buried quickly (usually within a day), and laid to rest in churchyards or significant locations. Outlaws couldn't be buried in consecrated ground—their deaths were unmarked, their graves forgotten.

Robin's burial at the edge of the forest, marked by his last arrow's flight, creates a legend instead of a forgotten grave. The choice of burial place matters: not in a church controlled by corrupt clergy, not in a town that rejected him, but at the boundary between civilization and wildness—where he always belonged.

informationalTexts.title: How Legends Survive
informationalTexts.text:
The Robin Hood legend has survived for over 700 years through ballads, plays, and stories. Why? Because it addresses timeless themes: rich vs. poor, justice vs. law, freedom vs. security, individual vs. authority. Every generation reinterprets the legend for its own time.

Historians debate whether Robin Hood was real. Some evidence suggests several real outlaws might have inspired the legend. But historical accuracy misses the point: Robin Hood is real as an idea. He represents the hope that someone will stand up to injustice, that the powerful can be challenged, and that one person with courage can make a difference.

writingPrompts.prompt: Robin makes a life-changing mistake because of his pride and temper. Write about a time when an emotion (anger, pride, fear, excitement) led you to make a bad decision. What happened? What did you learn?

Robin creates rules for his band: rob from the rich, give to the poor, never harm women or children, everyone has a voice. If you were creating a code of behavior for a group, what would your top three rules be? Explain why each rule matters.
Robin loses the bridge fight but gains a loyal friend. Write about a situation where losing, failing, or being wrong led to something good. What did you gain from that experience?

Robin knows the archery contest is a trap but goes anyway, saying "if I don't go, he wins." Write about a time when you had to face something difficult or risky even though it would have been easier to avoid it. What made you decide to go forward?

Will Scarlet loses his home and wealth but finds a new family with Robin's band. Write about a time when losing something (moving away, losing a friend, ending an activity) led to finding something new and valuable.
The tinker was hunting Robin for reward money but becomes his friend instead. Write about a time when your first impression of someone was wrong. How did your opinion change, and what caused it?

Friar Tuck left the church because he couldn't tolerate corruption and hypocrisy. Write about a time when you had to stand up for what was right even though it cost you something. What made you do it?
Robin is a master of disguise—he can become anyone. If you could disguise yourself and walk unrecognized through your school or community for a day, what would you want to see or learn? Why?
The Merry Men rescue Allan and Ellen's wedding. Write about a time when someone helped you in a difficult situation, or when you helped someone else. What happened, and how did it feel?

Robin's pride and love of showing off keep getting him into danger. Everyone has a quality that is both a strength and a weakness. What's yours? Give specific examples of how it helps you and how it sometimes causes problems.

Guy of Gisbourne fights without honor, mercy, or rules—just violence. Write about the difference between fighting for something (a cause, a principle, justice) versus fighting just to win or hurt others. Why does motivation matter?
Marian hides her identity and fights Robin to test him. Write about a time when you had to prove yourself to someone important to you. What did you do, and how did they react?

journalPrompts.prompt: Robin gives his band clear rules about who they can rob and who they must protect. Do you think stealing from the rich to give to the poor is justified? Why or why not? Are there situations where breaking the law might be the right thing to do?
After losing the fight, Robin laughs and immediately asks Little John to join his band. What does this tell you about Robin as a leader? What qualities make someone a good leader?

The Sheriff sets a trap specifically designed around Robin's vanity about archery. What does this teach us about how weaknesses can be used against us? How can we protect ourselves from people who know our weaknesses?

Will Scarlet refuses to change out of his red outfit even though everyone else wears green. Why do you think he does this? Is it important to maintain your individual identity even when you're part of a group?
The tinker was cheerful and friendly even while planning to capture Robin for money. Can someone be a good person even if they're doing something wrong? Where do you draw the line?
Friar Tuck is both a holy man and a skilled fighter. Can someone be both religious and violent? Are there situations where fighting is justified even for someone who preaches peace?
Robin constantly uses tricks and disguises to outsmart his enemies. Is trickery and deception wrong, or does it depend on who you're deceiving and why?
Ellen was being forced to marry someone she didn't love to pay her father's debt. Do children owe their parents absolute obedience, or are there times when it's right to refuse?

Marian says Robin is getting reckless because his pride makes him show off. If someone you cared about was making dangerous decisions, would you tell them? How do you balance caring about someone with respecting their choices?

The Sheriff's second trap comes closer to catching Robin than the first. Why do you think it's harder for Robin to escape this time? What does this teach us about how people learn from their failures?

Marian proves herself equal to any man in the band. Why do you think it was important to include a character like her in the story? Do stories have a responsibility to challenge unfair rules and stereotypes?

The Bishop travels with gold collected from poor people while living in luxury himself. This is legal but immoral. What's the difference between something being legal and something being right? Can you think of modern examples?
